query: muscles of larynx innervated by recurrent laryngeal nerve

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The muscles of the larynx are categorized as intrinsic or extrinsic. Intrinsic muscles function in phonation, while extrinsic muscles produce gross movements of the larynx.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The inferior laryngeal branch of the RLN innervates all intrinsic muscles of the larynx except for the cricothyroid muscle, which is supplied by the SLN. Additionally, the RLN carries general visceral sensory fibers from the region inferior to the glottis and sends branches to the inferior constrictor and cricopharyngeus muscles before entering the larynx.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The RLN is another branch of the vagus nerve. The left RLN loops inferior to the aortic arch and courses posterior to the ligamentum arteriosum. The right RLN arises from the vagus nerve as it passes posterior to the root of the right lung, looping around the right subclavian artery before ascending. Both RLNs travel superiorly along the lateral surfaces of the trachea and esophagus within the tracheoesophageal groove, passing posterior to the lobes of the thyroid gland. As these nerves enter the larynx, they course posterior to the cricothyroid joint and pass through fibers of the inferior constrictor muscles of the pharynx. At this point, the RLN transitions into the inferior laryngeal nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Surgical Considerations -- Nerve Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Monitoring the RLN and SLN is essential during neck procedures, such as thyroid lobectomy and thyroidectomy. [17] An indirect laryngoscopy serves as a crucial tool for assessing RLN integrity both before and after surgery. In cases of vocal fold dysfunction, reinnervation techniques may be applied to the abductors and tensors using a nerve-muscle pedicle. However, this approach is only viable if the vocal fold is not fixed.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx receives innervation from the superior laryngeal nerve (SLN), recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN), and sympathetic fibers (see Image . Laryngeal Nerves). [8] [9] [10] [11] The SLN branches from the vagus nerve approximately 2.5 cm below the base of the skull and divides into internal and external branches. The internal laryngeal nerve provides sensory and autonomic innervation to the mucosa superior to the glottis, including the superior portion of the laryngeal cavity, the epiglottis, and the superior surface of the vocal folds. Visceral afferents from the epiglottis also contribute to taste perception. Preganglionic parasympathetic fibers travel via the internal laryngeal nerve as well. The external laryngeal nerve supplies motor innervation and visceral efferents to the cricothyroid muscle.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Physiologic Variants -- Nerve Variation
retrieved_docs.content: Innervation of the laryngeal structures can vary, as seen in the phenomenon of laryngeal synkinesis. [13] This condition involves abnormal laryngeal innervation that becomes evident following trauma to the RLN. During nerve regeneration, motor axons from the RLN may exit their original pathway and follow branching nerves to establish new motor connections. Nearby nerve fibers can also sprout toward and reinnervate previously paralyzed intrinsic laryngeal muscles. These fibers may originate from the internal branch of the SLN, vagal branches of the pharyngeal constrictor muscles, or parasympathetic, sympathetic, and intralaryngeal branches. Laryngeal synkinesis is classified based on specific laryngeal and phonatory movement patterns.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Muscles -- Extrinsic Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Several muscles elevate the pharyngeal walls, including the middle pharyngeal constrictor, stylopharyngeus, and palatopharyngeus. Muscles that contribute to laryngeal elevation include the stylohyoid, hyoglossus, geniohyoid, mylohyoid, and digastric muscles. The omohyoid and sternohyoid muscles are responsible for laryngeal depression.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Muscles -- Extrinsic Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The extrinsic muscles of the larynx are found in bilateral pairs and contribute to the gross movement of the larynx. The innervation of these muscles varies, involving the ansa cervicalis, trigeminal nerve, facial nerve, glossopharyngeal nerve, and hypoglossal nerve. The pharyngeal constrictors and palatopharyngeus receive innervation from the glossopharyngeal, vagus, and spinal accessory nerves through the pharyngeal plexus.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Muscles -- Extrinsic Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Several extrinsic muscles attach directly to the larynx. The sternothyroid muscle connects the anterolateral aspect of the thyroid cartilage to the sternum, contributing to laryngeal depression. The thyrohyoid muscle inserts superior and medial to the sternothyroid muscle on the thyroid cartilage, causing laryngeal elevation. The inferior pharyngeal constrictor muscle attaches anteriorly along the lateral regions of the thyroid and cricoid cartilages, extending superiorly and posteriorly to meet with opposing fibers at the posterior median raphe of the pharynx. This muscle elevates the larynx and provides the only direct connection between the larynx and the skull.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Muscles -- Extrinsic Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Extrinsic muscles that do not attach directly to the larynx can influence its position by altering the placement of the hyoid bone or by connecting it to the walls of the pharynx. Some of these muscles contribute to laryngeal elevation, while others facilitate laryngeal depression.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Laryngeal Muscles -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The internal, external, and recurrent laryngeal nerves are critical for voice production and airway protection. These nerves are vulnerable to injury during neck and thyroid surgeries.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Muscles -- Intrinsic Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Unlike the other intrinsic muscles, the cricothyroid muscle does not attach to the arytenoid cartilage. Instead, this muscle runs along the lateral cricoid cartilage, with 2 bellies oriented superior-inferior. The superior belly attaches to the inferior portion of the thyroid lamina, while the inferior belly attaches to the inferior horn of the thyroid cartilage. These muscles function to lengthen the vocal folds.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Larynx Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The laryngeal muscles receive innervation from two nerves, the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve and the recurrent laryngeal nerve. These nerves both arise from the vagus nerve (cranial nerve X) and provide both motor and sensory innervation to the intrinsic muscles of the larynx, excluding the interarytenoid muscles. The cricothyroid muscle is innervated solely by the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve while the recurrent laryngeal provides motor innervates to the other intrinsic laryngeal muscles. The internal branch of superior laryngeal nerve supplies sensory to the mucosa of the larynx. The nucleus ambiguous within the brain stem provides the motoneurons to the intrinsic laryngeal muscles while the hypoglossal nucleus provides the motoneurons to extrinsic muscles. [5] The recurrent laryngeal branch of the vagus nerve descends in the neck and on the left side loops around the aorta before ascending and entering the cricothyroid membrane to innervate the muscles. On the right the nerve loops around the subclavian and ascends to enter the larynx. Non-recurrent anatomic variants on the right do exist.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Larynx -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx receives innervation from the inferior laryngeal nerve, the recurrent laryngeal nerve, and the superior laryngeal nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Muscles -- Intrinsic Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The posterior cricoarytenoid muscle extends from the cricoid cartilage to the muscular process of each arytenoid, serving as the only muscle pair that abducts the vocal folds. The lateral cricoarytenoid muscle extends from the cricoid cartilage to the muscular process of the arytenoid cartilage, functioning in vocal fold adduction. The thyroarytenoid muscle extends from the angle of the thyroid cartilage to the arytenoid cartilage, pulling the arytenoid anteriorly to relax and approximate the vocal folds. The vocalis muscle runs along the lateral aspect of the vocal ligament, shortening the vocal folds.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx is a flexible structure with a cartilaginous core, interconnecting membranes, and associated musculature. The larynx is positioned at the midline between the digestive and respiratory tracts. This structure houses the vocal cords and produces phonation. The larynx also facilitates airway protection and regulates intrathoracic and intraabdominal pressures. The anatomical position, composition, musculature, and innervation of the larynx contribute to its functions. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, indicating that these examinations focus on assessing the structure and function of the larynx (often in conjunction with the adjacent trachea) as part of otolaryngologic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2332
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly associated with the larynx, indicating that the larynx is the primary anatomical focus of these examinations. In addition, the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑OR therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, meaning that it can be involved secondarily in various non‑operative respiratory treatments. Understanding that the larynx functions both as the main target for diagnostic evaluation of the larynx and trachea and as an indirect site for additional respiratory therapies is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx and are organized as a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum. According to the indirect_procedure_site_of relationship, the larynx also serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. This dual classification highlights the larynx’s central role both as the primary anatomical focus of specific diagnostic interventions and as a secondary site affected by broader non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, emphasizing its importance in the overall assessment and management of respiratory tract conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2333
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of the larynx includes the subsequent step of تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway), indicating that airway management is an integral component of the laryngeal injection procedure. This relationship underscores that when clinicians perform a laryngeal injection, they also consider establishing an oral‑pharyngeal airway to maintain airway patency and support effective ventilation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 789
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are situated within the broader category of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum, indicating that examinations of the larynx and trachea are considered part of the general set of respiratory‑tract diagnostic activities. Therapeutic interventions such as Injection of larynx and Injection of locally‑acting therapeutic substance into trachea are each classified as throat surgery, reflecting that these injections are surgical procedures performed on the upper airway. Although throat surgery is primarily a therapeutic classification, it is linked to the larger diagnostic group of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum, highlighting that certain interventions on the larynx and trachea can serve both diagnostic and therapeutic roles. Recognizing this overlap helps clinicians select appropriate procedures and coordinate care for patients requiring evaluation or treatment of laryngeal and tracheal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2325
retrieved_docs.content: The trachea serves as the anatomical site for tracheostomy procedures, and these procedures may be performed as either temporary or permanent interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2158
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of larynx and injection of locally-acting therapeutic substance into trachea are both classified as throat surgery, representing surgical interventions that involve delivering agents directly into the laryngeal or tracheal structures of the airway.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1315
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of larynx is classified as a type of throat surgery, indicating that this procedure involves an injection administered directly into the laryngeal structures as part of a surgical intervention on the throat.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2381
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of larynx is classified as a throat surgery and encompasses the specific procedure تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي, which is listed as a child of Injection of larynx. In addition, Injection of locally-acting therapeutic substance into trachea is also categorized as a throat surgery. Both interventions involve therapeutic injections within the upper airway and represent distinct subtypes of throat surgery, illustrating the variety of surgical options available for managing laryngeal and tracheal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1263
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly linked to the larynx, indicating that examinations and assessments targeting these structures focus on the laryngeal anatomy. The relationship identified as “indirect_procedure_site_of” connects the larynx to the broader category “other non-or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system,” meaning that the larynx can serve as an indirect site for non‑operative therapeutic interventions within the respiratory system. Within this category, the relationship “has child” designates the specific procedure “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” (placement of an oral and pharyngeal airway) as a subordinate or child procedure. Consequently, the placement of an oral/pharyngeal airway is classified under “other non-or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system” and may involve the larynx indirectly. Recognizing these interconnected relationships aids clinicians in coordinating diagnostic evaluations of the larynx with therapeutic airway management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 508
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is categorized as a placement of an oral and pharyngeal airway (تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي). Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, and the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Injection of larynx includes, as a subordinate concept, the placement of an oral and pharyngeal airway, and the broader group of other non‑therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system also contains this airway‑placement procedure. Together, these connections highlight how airway management interventions such as intubation, mechanical ventilation, and oral‑pharyngeal airway placement are organized within the taxonomy of respiratory procedures, with the larynx functioning as a central anatomical focus for both diagnostic and non‑therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1313
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect site for various other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Within this broader category, the specific intervention described as تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway) is classified as a child procedure. Recognizing that the larynx may be indirectly involved in such non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions, including the insertion of an oral‑pharyngeal airway, is important for clinicians when assessing airway management and potential procedural complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 850
retrieved_docs.content: bronchitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي) when it involves nerve findings, and muscular atrophy/Bell’s palsy (وهن عضلي وبيل), which reflect the nerves’ integral role in motor and sensory function. Together, these relationships delineate how a diverse spectrum of nervous‑system disorders is organized by their pathogenic mechanisms and anatomical involvement, providing a framework essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1907
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. The specific intervention “Injection of larynx” includes as a subordinate step the procedure “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway). Likewise, the broader category “other non‑or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system” also lists “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” as a child procedure. This demonstrates that placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway is a common subprocedure both within targeted laryngeal injections and within general non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions, underscoring its relevance to airway management across multiple procedural contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 857
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of larynx and injection of locally‑acting therapeutic substance into trachea are both identified as forms of throat surgery. The category “other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum” is classified as a subclass within the broader throat‑surgery group, indicating that it groups procedures that examine the respiratory tract and mediastinum. Tracheoscopy via tracheostomy is considered the same as the “other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum,” and therefore it too falls under the umbrella of throat surgery. This hierarchy shows that therapeutic injections to the larynx or trachea and endoscopic diagnostic techniques performed through a tracheostomy are organized together as throat‑surgery procedures, reflecting their shared anatomical focus and relevance to patient care.
